coredata.dc:title: One-pot synthesis of BN-CDs fluorescent probe for determination of FOX-7
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Shasha
Lv, Jingjing
Pei, Fubin
Wu, Yi
Hao, Qingli
Tong, Zhaoyang
Lei, Wu
He, Guangyu
coredata.dc:description: A highly selective fluorescence sensor based on boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (BN-CDs) was firstly constructed for the determination of 1,1-diamino-2,2-dinitroethylene (FOX-7). The BN-CDs fluorescent probe was prepared by a simple, green, and low-cost one-pot hydrothermal carbonization method. The BN-CDs with good dispersion had strong fluorescence property, and the particle size was about 3 nm. The Fourier transform infrared spectrum and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy showed that B and N elements were successfully co-doped into CDs. Under the optimal experiment condition, BN-CDs fluorescent probe had a good linear relationship with the concentrations of FOX-7 in the range of 0–80 μM, and the limit of detection was 0.41 μM. The fluorescence of BN-CDs decreased with the increase of FOX-7 concentration, which was ascribed to inner filter effect. The fluorescent sensor has high sensitivity and good selectivity, which can be successfully applied in actual water samples with satisfactory recovery.
coredata.dcterms:subject: BN-CDs
Fluorescence quenching
Determination
FOX-7

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing property of the fluorescent probe

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of BN-CDs with different magnification.
(A) XPS survey spectrum of BN-CDs. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s (B), N 1s (C) and B 1s (D).
(A) 3D fluorescence spectra of BN-CDs. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of BN-CDs at various excitation wavelengths.
The effect of pH for the fluorescence quenching of BN-CDs in the presence of 10 μM FOX-7.

(A) Fluorescence response of BN-CDs with various concentrations of FOX-7 (0, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 10, 15, 20, 30, 40, 50, 80 μM); (B) Linear relationship between fluorescence intensity F0/F of BN-CDs and the concentration of FOX-7.

(A) Fluorescence emission (a) and excitation spectra (b) of BN-CDs, UV–vis absorption spectrum of FOX-7 (c). (B) Time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of BN-CDs with various concentrations of FOX-7.
(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of BN-CDs with various concentrations of FOX-7. (B) Selectivity of BN-CDs.
The synthesis process of fluorescent BN-CDs and diagram for detection FOX-7 by IFE.
Determination of FOX-7 in real samples.

head.ce:title: One-pot synthesis of BN-CDs fluorescent probe for determination of FOX-7

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing, 210094, China

Nanjing University of Science and Technology

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Technology, Changzhou University, 213164, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A highly selective fluorescence sensor based on boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (BN-CDs) was firstly constructed for the determination of 1,1-diamino-2,2-dinitroethylene (FOX-7). The BN-CDs fluorescent probe was prepared by a simple, green, and low-cost one-pot hydrothermal carbonization method. The BN-CDs with good dispersion had strong fluorescence property, and the particle size was about 3 nm. The Fourier transform infrared spectrum and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy showed that B and N elements were successfully co-doped into CDs. Under the optimal experiment condition, BN-CDs fluorescent probe had a good linear relationship with the concentrations of FOX-7 in the range of 0–80 μM, and the limit of detection was 0.41 μM. The fluorescence of BN-CDs decreased with the increase of FOX-7 concentration, which was ascribed to inner filter effect. The fluorescent sensor has high sensitivity and good selectivity, which can be successfully applied in actual water samples with satisfactory recovery.

ce:section.ce:para: Sensitive detection of high-energy substances is essential for world safety. 1,1-diamino-2,2-dinitroethylene (FOX-7) is a new type of insensitive explosive with high energy, which possesses good thermal stability [1]. The high explosive performance of FOX-7 is close to that of hexogen (RDX), and its sensitivity is equivalent to that of triaminotrinitrobenzene (TATB). It is considered a promising high energy density material. From the perspective of theory and practice, FOX-7 has been applied in many fields due to its low sensitivity, better thermal stability and relatively high safety [2]. However, due to the similarity of nitro compounds, it is undoubtedly difficult to detect FOX-7. Therefore, it is urgent to develop a simple and fast method for FOX-7 detection.

Currently, there are many methods focus on detecting explosives, including mass spectrometry [3], Raman spectroscopy [4], and high performance liquid chromatography [5]. However, these methods suffer from the lack of professional operators, time-consuming and high cost. In recent years, fluorescence analysis has attracted increasing attention due to its advantages such as simple operation, high sensitivity and rapid response [6]. Therefore, it is significant to find green fluorescent probes for the selective determination of FOX-7.

Carbon-based quantum dots (CDs) have attracted extensive interest of researchers recently, which have many advantages including low toxicity, good biocompatibility, simple preparation, admirable water solubility, and easy functionalization [7]. Doping is considered to be an effective method for adjusting surface defects and changing the properties of materials, including carbon-based materials [8–11]. Boron (B), nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), and phosphorus (P) are the most commonly used heteroatoms for doping carbon-based materials. Fu et al. [12] established a rapid and universal sensor for detecting ovalbumin (OVA) based on Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) by using nitrogen, oxygen, phosphorus co-doped carbon dots (NOP-CDs) and graphene oxide (GO). The sensor has high sensitivity and good selectivity, which can be successfully applied in actual samples for detecting OVA. Song et al. [13] synthesized multifunctional N, S co-doped CDs (N, S-CQDs) by one-step hydrothermal method. N, S-CQDs have good ionic stability, good biocompatibility and fluorescence characteristics of temperature-sensitive. In addition, N, S-CQDs can be applied for selective detection of glutathione. Liu et al. [14] prepared nitrogen and boron co-doped CDs (NB-CQD) by one-pot hydrothermal method, using citric acid, borax and p-phenylenediamine as precursors. The characterization of NB-CQD revealed that N and B elements were successfully incorporated into the carbon skeleton, and functional groups containing N and B were formed on the surface of NB-CQD. The atomic radius of the adjacent elements B and N of carbon (C) in the periodic table of elements is similar to that of C, doping B and N can effectively adjust the physical and chemical properties of CDs and provide more active sites [15,16]. So the optical and electrical properties of the CDs can be effectively adjusted by heteroatom doping, thereby bringing a broader application prospect. The fluorescence sensing mechanisms includes FRET, photo induced electron transfer (PET), inner filter effect (IFE) and so on [17]. Among them, the most common sensing mechanisms based on CDs are IFE and FRET. The similarity between IEF and FRET is that the absorption spectrum of the receptor overlaps with the excitation or emission spectrum of the donor. The difference is that for FRET, when there is a receptor, the fluorescence lifetime of the donor will change, and a closer distance between the donor and the receptor is required [18,19]. But there is no report that CDs modified for fluorescent probe are used to detect FOX-7 based on IFE.

Here, boron and nitrogen co-doped CDs (BN-CDs) fluorescent probe was prepared by a one-pot hydrothermal carbonization method for selective detection of FOX-7 in aqueous solutions. The size of synthesized BN-CDs was about 3 nm with good water solubility and fluorescence properties. FOX-7 has a strong quenching effect on the fluorescence of BN-CDs, which was ascribed to IFE. IFE does not limit the distance between the fluorophore and absorber [20] and provides considerable flexibility and simplicity, which makes the sensor easier to design. In this work, BN-CDs with strong blue fluorescence and FOX-7 are used as the fluorophore and absorber of IFE, respectively. The sensor had high sensitivity and selectivity. The synthesis process of fluorescent BN-CDs and diagram for detection FOX-7 via IFE are shown in Scheme 1 .

Citric acid, ethylene diamine (EDA), boric acid, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and cadmium chloride (CdCl2) were obtained by Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Hydrochloric acid (HCl), calcium chloride (CaCl2), and mercury dichloride (HgCl2) were provide by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All reagents used in this experiment are of analytical purity. All reagents were of analytical grade. FOX-7, 3-nitro-1,2,4-triazol-5-one (NTO), 2,4-dinitroanisole (DNAN), trinitrotoluene (TNT), o-nitro Phenylphenol (o-NP), octogen (HMX), TATB, and RDX were provided by Nanjing University of Science and Technology (Nanjing, Jiangsu).

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were conducted on JEM-2100F (Japan Electronics Co., Ltd.). Fourier transformed infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was obtained by a Nicolet isl0 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). The UV–vis absorption spectra were measured on a UV-220 spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded by Thermo ESCALAB 250 spectrometer. All fluorescence spectra measurements were recorded on a FL3-TCSPC fluorescence spectrophotometer (HORIBA Jobin Yvon, France).

BN-CDs were prepared according to previously reported methods with minor modifications. The specific experimental process was as follows: dissolve 0.42 g citric acid, 0.5 mL EDA and 42 mg boric acid in 80 mL water by stirring. Then, the mixed solution was transferred to a 200 mL polytetrafluoroethylene-lined stainless steel autoclave at 200 °C for 5 h. After the autoclave was cooled to room temperature naturally, the aqueous solution of BN-CDs was obtained, which was stored at 4 °C for further use.

The prepared BN-CDs were diluted 100 times with deionized water, and different concentrations of FOX-7 were sequentially added. All fluorescence experiments were measured under the excitation wavelength of 370 nm, a slit of 1 nm, and a step size of 0.5 nm.

As shown in Fig. 1 , the morphology of BN-CDs at different magnifications were directly visualized by TEM. BN-CDs presented spherical particles with good dispersibility. The particle size was mainly distributed in the range of 2 nm–5 nm, and the average particle size was about 3 nm.

To study the structure of the BN-CDs, the FT-IR spectra were carried out. As illustrated in Fig. 2 , the broad absorption band at around 3051 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of N–H. The absorption peaks at 1650 cm−1 and 1552 cm−1 were attributed to C–O stretching vibration and N–H/CC bending vibration, respectively. The absorption peaks at 1395 and 1047 cm−1 were ascribed to C–N and C–O stretching vibrations. The absorption peak at 1485 cm−1 was attributed to O–H bending vibration. The peak at 1334 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of B–O. The result indicated that the surface of BN-CDs was rich in hydroxyl groups, amine groups and oxygen-containing groups. BN-CDs had good water solubility as hydroxyl groups and better fluorescence property due to the formation of more defects [21].

To further study the composition and possible chemical structure of BN-CDs, XPS spectral scanning (Fig. 3 ) was performed. As shown in Fig. 3A, the XPS survey spectrum of BN-CDs showed four peaks at 190.0, 284.0, 398.0, and 530.0 eV corresponding to B 1s, C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 3B) showed four peaks at 283.8, 284.9, 285.6 and 286.7 eV, which were assigned to C–B, C–C, C–O, and CN/CO, respectively [22]. Fig. 3C illustrated the high-resolution N 1s spectrum corresponding to pyridinic N (398.2 eV), C–N–B (399.0 eV), pyrrolic N (399.9 eV), and graphitic N (401.3 eV). The B 1s spectrum (Fig. 3D) was deconvoluted into three peaks at 190.6 eV (BC2O), 191.3 eV (B–N) and 192.0 eV (BCO2) [23]. The result demonstrated the successful co-doping of B/N heteroatoms in BN-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing property of the fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: The three-dimensional (3D) fluorescence spectrum of BN-CDs is shown in Fig. 4 A, and the top of the figure is its corresponding contour map. It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of BN-CDs changed with different excitation wavelengths, and the maximum excitation and emission wavelength ranges were mainly concentrated in the red area at the top. The optimum excitation and emission wavelengths of BN-CDs are 370 nm and 435 nm, respectively.

In order to further confirm the optimal excitation wavelength, the fluorescence emission spectra at various excitation wavelengths were measured (Fig. 4B). It can be seen from the inset that the BN-CDs fluorescence intensity gradually increased with excitation wavelength ranging from 340 nm to 370 nm. The fluorescence intensity reached the maximum at the excitation wavelength of 370 nm. However, the fluorescence intensity of BN-CDs decreased when the excitation wavelength was longer than 370 nm. So 370 nm was the optimal excitation wavelength. The result was consistent with the 3D fluorescence spectra. The emission wavelength displayed a slight red-shift as the excitation wavelength increased. These results showed that BN-CDs fluorescent probe had excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior, which may be related to the trap states, wide size distribution and quantum confinement effect of BN-CDs. It can be seen from the FT-IR spectrum that there are rich functional groups on the surface of BN-CDs, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, confirming that BN-CDs have abundant trap states. And different energy levels are formed due to heterogeneity of particle size [24,25].

The optimum pH of BN-CDs solution for detecting FOX-7 was investigated. It was found that the prepared BN-CDs could be effectively quenched by FOX-7. The fluorescence quenching degree (F0/F) of BN-CDs by 10 μM FOX-7 at different pH (5.0–9.0) was further studied. Fig. 5 displayed the relationship between F0/F and pH, where F0 and F represented the fluorescence signals in the absence and presence of FOX-7, respectively. It can be seen that F0/F reached the maximum at pH = 7.0. When the pH value increased further, the F0/F decreased. Therefore, the optimal pH for FOX-7 detection was 7.0.

The fluorescence intensity of BN-CDs with various concentrations of FOX-7 was recorded (Fig. 6 A). As the concentrations of FOX-7 increased (0, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 10, 15, 20, 30, 40, 50 and 80 μM, respectively), the fluorescence intensity of BN-CDs solution gradually decreased. Fig. 6B exhibited the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity F0/F of BN-CDs solution and the concentration of FOX-7. It can be seen that F0/F had a good linear relationship with FOX-7 concentration in the range of 0–80 μM. The linear correlation can be described as F0/F = 0.022CFOX-7+1.025 (R2 = 0.997). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 0.41 μM according to the calculation formula LOD = 3Sd/k, where, Sd was the standard deviation, and k was the slope of the fitted linear curve.

Fig. 7 A showed the emission (curve a) and excitation spectra (curve b) of BN-CDs and the UV–vis absorption spectrum (curve c) of FOX-7. It can be seen that the absorption spectrum of FOX-7 largely overlapped the excitation spectrum of BN-CDs, which indicated that the fluorescence quenching of BN-CDs was caused by IFE or FRET. In order to further explore the mechanism of fluorescence quenching of BN-CDs, the fluorescence lifetimes of BN-CDs with various concentrations of FOX-7 were further studied (Fig. 7B). It is well known that the lifetime of the fluorophore will not be changed by a quencher during the IFE process, which is different from FRET [19]. As shown in Fig. 7B, the time-resolved fluorescence decay curve of BN-CDs with the increase of FOX-7 concentration remained unchanged, which indicated that the significant decrease of BN-CDs fluorescence intensity was ascribed to IFE rather than FRET, and the fluorescence quenching process between BN-CDs and FOX-7 was static.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of BN-CDs in the presence of various concentrations of FOX-7 were displayed in Fig. 8 A. The concentrations of FOX-7 from bottom to top are 0, 5, 10, 20, 30, 50, and 80 μM, respectively. It can be seen that the UV–vis absorption spectrum of BN-CDs have only a clear peak at 349 nm before FOX-7 was added, and the intensity of the absorption peak gradually increased with the increasing concentration of FOX-7, but the absorption peak remained unchanged. It is suggested that there is no formation of new substances, and there is an IFE process between BN-CDs and FOX-7 [22].

In order to study the selectivity of the fluorescent probe, we explored the fluorescence response of BN-CDs to FOX-7 by recording fluorescence intensity changes in the presence of possible interferences including 500 μM of heavy metal ions Ca2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, and 50 μM of NTO, DNAN, TNT, o-NP, HMX, TATB and RDX. As shown in Fig. 8B, metal ions and other nitro-based explosives had negligible interference effect on the fluorescence quenching of BN-CDs. The results indicated that the constructed fluorescence sensor had good selectivity for the detection of FOX-7.

The stability of BN-CDs fluorescent probe was further determined. The change of fluorescence intensity of BN-CDs after one week was shown in Fig. 9 , which changed little. It was thus clear that BN-CDs had good stability.

In order to assess the practicality of FOX-7 detection by the proposed method, the standard addition method was used to detect the FOX-7 in tap water and lake water. The results are shown in Table 1 . It was found that the recoveries ranged from 94.0% to 108.0% with the relative standard deviation between 0.38% and 3.27%, which were satisfactory. The results demonstrated that the BN-CDs fluorescent probe can be applied in real samples for analysis of FOX-7 successfully.

In this study, a fluorescent method for FOX-7 detection was proposed based on BN-CDs. BN-CDs fluorescent probe was prepared by the one-pot hydrothermal carbonization method. The synthesized BN-CDs have a particle size of about 3 nm and strong fluorescence property, which are easy to prepare and have low preparation cost. FOX-7 has a strong quenching effect on the fluorescence of BN-CDs. It could be concluded that IFE led to the quenching of BN-CDs fluorescence. Under the optimal condition, the liner detection range is from 0 to 80 μM with a LOD of 0.41 μM. The sensor exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity. In addition, the sensor can be applied in actual samples with satisfactory recoveries.

Shasha Feng: Investigation, Conceptualization, Validation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Jingjing Lv: Validation, Investigation. Fubin Pei: Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Yi Wu: Data curation, Methodology. Qingli Hao: Funding acquisition. Zhaoyang Tong: Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Wu Lei: Supervision, Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Guangyu He: Supervision, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51872140, 51972173), Foundation of State Key Laboratory of NBC Protection for Civilian (SKLNBC2020-07), High-end Foreign Expert Introduction Program (G20200010151), and Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Technology (BM2012110), China. We also thank the support of the Analysis and Test Center, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, for SEM, TEM and XPS data collection.
